header.subtitle: Module 5: Secure Workspace - Guardrails & CONSTITUTION.md
sec.incident.intro: This is not hypothetical. During our sessions, developers gave full autonomy to the AI — no rules, no limits, no confirmations. The result was destructive. Fortunately we had backups. But the scare was real.
sec.incident.card.1.title: Incident 1: Database
sec.incident.card.1.desc: Dev asked: "clean up the test data from the database". The AI interpreted it as unrestricted permission and ran DROP TABLE on multiple production tables without any confirmation.
sec.incident.card.2.title: Incident 2: Project Files
sec.incident.card.2.desc: Dev asked: "reorganize the project structure". The AI deleted 47 files it deemed "duplicates or unnecessary", including CI/CD configs and historical migrations.
sec.incident.lesson.quote: "The problem was not the AI. It was the absence of limits."
sec.incident.lesson.detail: The AI did exactly what was asked — in a more literal and sweeping way than the dev expected. Without explicit rules, there was no way to know where to stop.
sec.incident.term.danger.label: claude-code without CONSTITUTION.md
sec.incident.term.danger.dev: Dev: "Clean up the test data from the database."
sec.incident.term.danger.note: No confirmation requested. No rules violated — because no rules existed.
sec.incident.term.safe.label: claude-code with CONSTITUTION.md active
sec.incident.term.safe.dev: Dev: "Clean up the test data from the database."
sec.incident.term.safe.warning: ⚠️ CONSTITUTION.md prohibits DROP TABLE without explicit confirmation. Before proceeding:
sec.incident.term.safe.step2: 2. Do these tables have relevant data in production?
sec.incident.term.safe.step3: 3. I prefer using TRUNCATE (preserves structure) instead of DROP. I can create a reversible script.
sec.incident.term.safe.awaiting: Waiting for confirmation and clarification before executing any action.
sec.folder.intro: The .claude/ folder is the central nervous system of your secure workspace. Click on each file or folder to understand its purpose.
sec.constitution.intro: The Constitution defines what the AI can never do, what requires confirmation, and the mandatory code and architecture standards. It is imported into every conversation and serves as a contract between the team and the AI.
sec.constitution.never.intro: These actions are prohibited without explicit, written confirmation from the developer:
sec.constitution.never.card.1.title: DROP TABLE / TRUNCATE / DELETE without WHERE
sec.constitution.never.card.2.title: git push --force
sec.constitution.never.card.2.desc: On any branch, including feature branches
sec.constitution.never.card.3.title: rm -rf / delete files without backup
sec.constitution.never.card.4.title: Modify production .env
sec.constitution.never.card.5.title: Expose secrets / tokens in code
sec.constitution.never.card.5.desc: Hardcoded API keys, passwords, or tokens
sec.constitution.never.card.6.title: DROP DATABASE / global schema operations
sec.constitution.ask.intro: Before executing these actions, the AI must pause and get confirmation:
sec.constitution.ask.card.1.title: Alter database schema
sec.constitution.ask.card.2.title: Install global dependencies
sec.constitution.ask.card.3.title: Create files outside plan.md scope
sec.constitution.ask.card.3.desc: Any file not listed in the approved plan
sec.constitution.ask.card.4.title: Modify existing migrations
sec.constitution.ask.card.4.desc: Already-applied migrations must not be altered
sec.constitution.ask.card.5.title: Alter CI/CD pipelines
sec.constitution.ask.card.6.title: Alter authentication settings
sec.constitution.ask.card.6.desc: Any change to the auth/authorization flow
sec.constitution.must.intro: Standards that apply to all new code, no exceptions:
sec.constitution.must.card.1.title: TypeScript strict mode
sec.constitution.must.card.1.desc: No implicit any. No typing shortcuts.
sec.constitution.must.card.2.title: Tests for all new code
sec.constitution.must.card.2.desc: Unit tests for business logic, integration tests for critical flows
sec.constitution.must.card.3.title: Names in English, comments in PT-BR
sec.constitution.must.card.3.desc: Variables, functions, and files in English
sec.constitution.must.card.4.title: No console.log in production
sec.constitution.must.card.5.title: Environment variables via process.env
sec.constitution.must.card.6.title: Reversible migrations mandatory
sec.constitution.must.card.6.desc: Every up() must have a working down()
sec.constitution.arch.intro: Architectural decisions that the AI cannot bypass or ignore:
sec.constitution.arch.card.1.title: Feature Folders
sec.constitution.arch.card.1.desc: Code organized by domain, not by file type
sec.constitution.arch.card.2.title: Repository / Service / Controller separation
sec.constitution.arch.card.2.desc: No business logic in controllers or routes
sec.constitution.arch.card.3.title: Typed errors, not strings
sec.constitution.arch.card.3.desc: Use custom error classes, not throw "message"
sec.constitution.arch.card.4.title: Dependencies approved in TECH_STACK.md
sec.constitution.arch.card.4.desc: Do not install libs outside the approved inventory without discussion
sec.index.h2: CLAUDE.md as Index, Not a Dump
sec.index.intro: The CLAUDE.md is loaded in every conversation with Claude Code. Every unnecessary line inflates the context, increases cost, and distracts the model. The solution: turn it into a smart router.
sec.index.anti.title: ❌ Anti-pattern: Rule dump
sec.index.anti.desc: CLAUDE.md with 400+ lines of inline rules. Fully loaded in every conversation.
sec.index.correct.title: ✅ Correct: Smart router
sec.index.correct.desc: CLAUDE.md with ~20 lines. Imports only the essentials, routes the rest.
sec.hooks.h2: Hooks & Permissions: The Second Line of Defense
sec.hooks.intro: CONSTITUTION.md guides intent. Hooks block at execution. They are complementary layers, not substitutes. The .claude/settings.json is where you configure both.
sec.hooks.layer.constitution.title: CONSTITUTION.md
sec.hooks.layer.constitution.desc: AI context. The AI knows it should not do X — this influences reasoning before proposing an action. Can be overridden if explicitly instructed.
sec.hooks.layer.hooks.title: Hooks in settings.json
sec.hooks.layer.hooks.desc: Real execution. The hook intercepts before the command runs and can block it with exit 2. Does not depend on the AI — it is a technical barrier.
sec.hooks.permissions.title: Permissions in settings.json
sec.hooks.permissions.desc: Beyond hooks, Claude Code allows denying specific tools via permissions.deny:
sec.mcpskills.intro: Each extension layer of Claude Code brings benefits and new risk vectors. CONSTITUTION.md must cover all of them explicitly.
sec.mcpskills.mcp.danger.title: ❌ Dangerous configuration
sec.mcpskills.mcp.safe.title: ✅ Secure configuration
sec.mcpskills.mcp.rule: Rule for CONSTITUTION.md: "MCP servers may only use staging credentials with read-only permission. Never connect to production via MCP in a local environment."
sec.mcpskills.skills.danger.title: ❌ Skill with automatic destructive action
sec.mcpskills.skills.safe.title: ✅ Skill with confirmation step
sec.mcpskills.skills.audit: Quarterly skills audit: For each file in .claude/commands/, verify: who created it, what it does, whether it is still needed, and whether it has any irreversible action embedded.
sec.limits.intro: Well-configured guardrails reduce risks but do not eliminate them. Understanding the limits is just as important as implementing the protections.
sec.limits.card.1.title: Ambiguous instructions override any rule
sec.limits.card.1.desc: "Clean everything" is dangerous regardless of how many rules exist. CONSTITUTION.md protects against unintended actions, not against poorly worded instructions from the dev. The responsibility to be clear always falls on the human.
sec.limits.card.2.title: The AI can be convinced to ignore rules
sec.limits.card.2.desc: If the dev says "ignore CONSTITUTION.md for this case", the AI will likely comply. Context guardrails are not technical barriers — they are instructions. For real blocks, use hooks in settings.json.
sec.limits.card.3.title: Conflicting rules paralyze or confuse
sec.limits.card.3.desc: If CONSTITUTION.md says "never install dependencies" and CLAUDE.md says "optimize the environment", the AI will oscillate. Keep the files coherent and review for conflicts whenever a rule is changed.
sec.limits.card.4.title: Outdated rules are dangerous
sec.limits.card.4.desc: A 6-month-old CONSTITUTION.md may prohibit practices the team has already adopted, or fail to cover new risks introduced by new tools. Obsolete rules create a false sense of security.
sec.limits.card.5.title: Guardrails do not replace human review
sec.limits.card.5.desc: The AI can generate structurally correct code that follows all rules, but solves the wrong problem. Judging intent is still 100% human.
sec.limits.mantra.label: The Part 5 Mantra:
sec.limits.mantra.quote: "Guardrails don't make the AI safe. They make your limits explicit — and explicit is always better than implicit."
sec.nextsteps.intro: The demo files are available in demo/ in this repository. Copy, adapt, and use as a starting point.
sec.nextsteps.step.1.title: Create the .claude/rules/ structure with CONSTITUTION.md
sec.nextsteps.step.1.desc: Use the template from section 3. Adapt the rules to your project. Involve the team — the Constitution should represent collective decisions, not individual mandates.
sec.nextsteps.step.2.title: Rewrite CLAUDE.md as a minimal router
sec.nextsteps.step.2.desc: Replace the current content with the template from section 4. The only mandatory import is CONSTITUTION.md. Everything else is on demand.
sec.nextsteps.step.2.prompt: "Restructure our CLAUDE.md to have fewer than 30 lines, import only CONSTITUTION.md, and route other contexts to .claude/rules/README.md."
sec.nextsteps.step.3.title: Add the security hook to .claude/settings.json
sec.nextsteps.step.3.desc: Copy the template from demo/.claude/settings.json and adapt the blocking regex for the most critical operations in your project. Test with a safe command first.
sec.nextsteps.step.3.warning: Overly restrictive hooks can block legitimate operations. Start conservative and expand gradually.
sec.nextsteps.step.4.title: Schedule quarterly review of the Constitution + skills
sec.nextsteps.step.4.desc: 30 minutes each quarter with the team: are CONSTITUTION.md rules still valid? Are files in .claude/commands/ still necessary? Are active MCP servers still secure?
sec.nextsteps.demo.h3: Demo files available in this repository
